Question: Explain why the values of an increasing exponential function will eventually overtake the values of an increasing linear function.
Answer: Linear functions have a constant rate of change. Exponential functions increase based on a percent of the original.
o3 Answer:
Think of how each type of function “adds on” new value as x increases.

1.  How a linear function grows  
   Line:   f(x) = mx + b, with m > 0  
   Every time x goes up by 1, the function simply adds the same fixed amount m.  
   Next value = previous value + m.  
   The rate of change never alters; it is always m.

2.  How an exponential function grows  
   Exponential: g(x) = a·r^x, with a > 0 and r > 1  
   Every time x goes up by 1, the function multiplies by the same factor r.  
   Next value = previous value × r.  
   So the amount it increases by is not fixed; it is proportional to how large it already is.

3.  Compare their “extra” growth  
   Suppose at some x = k both functions happen to have the same value, i.e. f(k) = g(k).  
   • For the next step to x = k+1  
        – Linear adds m.  
        – Exponential multiplies by r, so it adds g(k)(r – 1).  
   • Because g(k) = f(k), the exponential’s one-step increase equals f(k)(r – 1).  
   • Choose any r > 1; eventually x becomes large enough that f(k) itself exceeds m/(r – 1).  
     Once that happens, g(k)(r – 1) > m, meaning the exponential’s single-step rise is already bigger than the constant rise of the line. From then on the exponential pulls farther ahead every step.

4.  Another quick mathematical view  
   Compare derivatives:  
      f ′(x) = m (constant)  
      g ′(x) = a·r^x ln r = g(x) ln r  
   Since g(x) grows without bound while f ′(x) stays fixed, there exists an X where g ′(X) > m. Once the exponential’s slope exceeds the line’s slope, the two graphs can cross at most once more; after that point the exponential stays above forever.

5.  Intuitive summary  
   Linear growth adds the same number each step; exponential growth adds a number that keeps getting larger each step because it is a fixed percentage of an ever-growing total. So no matter how flat the exponential starts, continual percentage-based compounding ultimately overtakes any constant-rate increase, and the exponential function will surpass—and keep outpacing—the linear function.
o4-mini Answer:
An increasing linear function grows by adding the same fixed amount every time you increase its input by 1—for example, if g(x)=mx+b then each time x goes up by 1, g goes up by exactly m.  An exponential function, by contrast, grows by multiplying its current value by the same factor every time you increase the input by 1—for example, f(x)=a^x (with a>1) satisfies  
    f(x+1)=a·f(x).  

Because the exponential’s “step‐increase” a·f(x)–f(x)=(a–1)·f(x) itself grows larger and larger (it’s always a fixed fraction of f(x), which is itself rising), eventually those increases exceed the constant step m of the linear function.  Once the exponential’s instantaneous rate of growth (its derivative or step size) exceeds the linear slope m, it never falls back, so its graph will cross above the linear graph and stay above it.  

In more analytic terms you can look at the difference  
    h(x)=a^x–(mx+b).  
Since h′(x)=(ln a)·a^x–m grows without bound (because a^x→∞) while m is fixed, beyond some point h′(x)>0 and so h(x) begins rising forever.  Hence for large enough x, a^x>mx+b.  

In plain English: a linear function adds the same amount each step; an exponential function adds an ever‐increasing amount (a fixed percentage of its current size), so given enough steps the exponential “snowball” always outpaces the constant “flat” increments of the line.

Question: The Oxford Dictionary defines the word nominal as a value that is “stated or expressed but not necessarily corresponding exactly to the real value.” 3 Develop a reasonable argument for why the term nominal rate is used to describe the annual percentage rate of an investment account that compounds interest.
Answer: When interest is compounded, the percentage of interest earned to principal ends up being greater than the annual percentage rate for the investment account. Thus, the annual percentage rate does not necessarily correspond to the real interest earned, which is the very definition of nominal .
o3 Answer:
Nominal literally means “in name only.”  
When a bank advertises “8 % compounded quarterly” or “12 % compounded monthly,” the percentage that appears in the advertisement is just a number that is attached to (named for) one calendar year; it is not the amount by which the money in the account will actually grow during that year.  

1. How the advertised (nominal) rate is obtained  
   • First the bank chooses a periodic rate i – the rate it will credit each compounding period.  
   • It then multiplies that periodic rate by the number m of compounding periods in a year and prints R = m · i as the “annual percentage rate” that appears in its brochure.

2. Why that figure is only “in name”  
   • The dollars that an investor really has at the end of the year are determined by compound growth, not by simple multiplication:  
     Ending value = Principal × (1 + i)ᵐ.  
   • The true or effective annual rate therefore is  
     r_eff = (1 + i)^m − 1.  
   • Except when m = 1, r_eff ≠ R.  The difference can be sizeable:  
     “12 % compounded monthly” → r_eff = (1 + 0.12/12)¹² − 1 ≈ 12.68 %.  
     The account actually grows 12.68 %, not 12 %.

3. Connection to the dictionary definition  
   Oxford: “Nominal – stated or expressed but not necessarily corresponding exactly to the real value.”  
   The advertised APR is exactly that: a number that is stated, printed, and used for comparison, yet it does not correspond to the real (effective) rate that determines the growth of the investment. It tells you nothing about how often the interest is added and therefore nothing about the interest-on-interest you will earn. You must convert the nominal rate to an effective rate before you know the “real value” of the investment’s yearly return.

Because this stated annual percentage rate is merely a name attached to the account and does not coincide with the actual percentage gain you receive, finance appropriately labels it the nominal rate.

### The Argument

The term **nominal rate** is a fitting descriptor for the Annual Percentage Rate (APR) of a compounding investment account because the APR represents the **stated, or named, interest rate** but fails to represent the **real, or effective, return** an investor actually earns over a year due to the effects of compounding.

Let's break this down using the dictionary definition provided:

**Definition:** "Nominal" means a value that is “stated or expressed but not necessarily corresponding exactly to the real value.”

Here is how the components of an investment account align with this definition:

---

#### 1. The "Stated or Expressed" Value: The APR

*   The **Annual Percentage Rate (APR)** is the simple, headline interest rate that a financial institution advertises for an account. For example, an account might be advertised with a "12% APR."
*   This 12% is the **"nominal rate."** It is the official, stated rate for a one-year period. It answers the simple question: "What is the name of the annual rate?"
*   Crucially, this rate *by itself* does not account for how many times the interest is calculated and paid into the account *within* that year.

#### 2. The "Real Value": The Effective Annual Rate (EAR) or APY

*   **Compounding** is the process of calculating interest not only on the initial principal but also on the accumulated interest from previous periods. This can happen semi-annually, quarterly, monthly, or even daily.
*   When interest is compounded more than once a year, you begin to earn "interest on your interest." This causes the investment to grow faster than the simple APR would suggest.
*   The **"real value"** of the interest earned over a full year is known as the **Effective Annual Rate (EAR)** or **Annual Percentage Yield (APY)**. This rate reflects the total interest earned after accounting for all compounding periods.

#### 3. The Discrepancy: Why the "Stated" Value is Not the "Real" Value

The discrepancy between the nominal rate (APR) and the real return (APY) is the core of the argument. The nominal rate only "corresponds exactly to the real value" in one specific, and uncommon, scenario: when interest is compounded only once per year. In all other cases, the real return is higher.

**Let's use a simple example:**

*   **Investment:** $1,000
*   **Nominal Rate (APR):** 12%

**Scenario A: Compounded Annually (The nominal rate = the real rate)**
*   Interest is calculated once at the end of the year.
*   Calculation: $1,000 * 12% = $120
*   End Balance: $1,120
*   In this case, the **stated rate (12%)** matches the **real return (12%)**.

**Scenario B: Compounded Monthly (The nominal rate ≠ the real rate)**
*   The 12% annual rate is divided across 12 months, so the monthly rate is 1% (12% / 12).
*   **Month 1:** $1,000 * 1% = $10 interest. New Balance: $1,010
*   **Month 2:** $1,010 * 1% = $10.10 interest. New Balance: $1,020.10
*   ...and so on for 12 months.
*   At the end of the year, the total balance will be **$1,126.83**.
*   The total interest earned is **$126.83**.

Now, let's compare the "stated" vs. "real" values:

*   The **"Stated/Expressed Value"** (Nominal Rate / APR) was **12%**.
*   The **"Real Value"** (Effective Rate / APY) is calculated as ($126.83 / $1,000) = **12.683%**.

### Conclusion

The 12% APR is the **nominal rate**—it is the rate "in name only." It is the officially stated annual figure but, because of monthly compounding, it does not reflect the true financial gain. The real return, or APY, is 12.683%.

Therefore, the term "nominal rate" is perfectly reasonable because the APR is **“stated or expressed but not necessarily corresponding exactly to the real value”** of the investment's annual growth whenever interest is compounded more than once per year.

Problem Statement: For the following exercises, identify whether the statement represents an exponential function. Explain.
Question: A population of bacteria decreases by a factor of $$\frac{1}{8}$$ every $$24$$ hours.
Answer: exponential; the population decreases by a proportional rate. .
o3 Answer:
Yes – the situation can be modeled with an exponential function.

Reasoning  
• An exponential function shows a constant multiplicative (percentage) change over equal time intervals.  
• Here, every 24-hour interval multiplies the population by the same factor, 1/8.  
• Let P0 be the initial number of bacteria and t the number of hours that have passed. After each 24-hour block the population is multiplied by 1/8, so

             P(t) = P0 (1/8)^(t/24)

This has the standard exponential form a · b^t (with a = P0 and b = (1/8)^(1/24) per hour, or b = 1/8 per 24-hour unit).  
Because the change is multiplicative and not additive, the statement indeed describes an exponential (specifically, exponential decay) function.
o4-mini Answer:
Yes.  “Decreases by a factor of 1/8 every 24 h” means that every 24-hour step you multiply by the constant 1/8, so the population satisfies an exponential‐decay law.  If P₀ is the initial population (at t = 0 hours), then for any t (in hours)

 P(t) = P₀·(1/8)^(t/24).

Equivalently, one can write it in the continuous‐time form

 P(t) = P₀·e^(kt),   where k = (ln (1/8))/24 = –(3 ln 2)/24.

Because the ratio P(t+24)/P(t) is always the constant 1/8, this is an exponential function.
